

CS 2430 Programming Project 3: Optimal selection\_PLO-CS-4  
Group 4: Ian Shoell, Korbin Ordiway, Kathleen Monahan, and Marcos Elgueta

**Introduction**

In reality, situations arise where one needs to make a choice of the most appropriate option combinations, taking into account some limitations. The issue arises in budget allocation, time management, resource allocation, and other spheres of human activity. The particular case that we address in this project is the knapsack problem. The idea behind this problem is that a certain value should be maximized subject to a single constraint. In our case, the set of possible actions is represented by several scientific experiments, each having some weight and rating value. The problem is to pick the optimal subset of these experiments in such a way that their total rating will be maximized under the condition that the total weight does not exceed 700 kg. We analyzed various algorithmic solutions for this problem, ranging from greedy methods to brute-force and dynamic programming approaches.

**Methods and Approach**  
Several algorithmic techniques were applied using the Java programming language in an attempt to examine their efficiency in maximizing the sum of ratings, considering a maximum allowable weight of 700kg. The said technique is simply a different way of making decisions.

	The **three greedy algorithms** have been implemented that build a solution by making locally optimal choices at each step.

* The **rating-first greedy algorithm** selects experiments with the highest ratings in decreasing order, picking those that fit within the available weight capacity. Although it aims to maximize value per selection, it does not always use weight capacity efficiently.  
* The **weight-first greedy algorithm** selects the lightest experiments first to maximize the number of selections and minimize the total weight, without considering the value of each experiment.  
* The **ratio-first greedy algorithm** makes its selections based on the largest value of the ratio rating/weight for each experiment. A value, calculated as the ratio between rating and weight, is determined for each experiment, and experiments are selected based on the decreasing ratio. Generally, the ratio-first greedy algorithm produces better results than both of the previous algorithms, but just like all others, it does not produce optimal solutions.

Selections under all three greedy algorithms cease when adding another experiment exceeds the 700kg weight restriction.

A **brute-force algorithm** was applied to get the real optimum solution. A brute force approach, where all the combinations of experiments would be tested, was applied. For instance, in this problem, we have 12 experiments; hence, we would have (2^{12} \= 4096\) subsets.

This is achieved through a bit mask, where each bit is used to show whether a particular experiment has been selected for the present subset. After that, all the subsets' weights and ratings are checked. All the subsets with more weight than 700kg are disregarded.

Afterwards, out of all the remaining subsets, the top three subsets with the highest rating are selected, from which the best one is finally chosen. Brute force guarantees correctness; however, it suffers from a time complexity that increases exponentially with the increase in the size of the input data.

	We used a **dynamic programming** algorithm to find the solution without looking at all possible combinations. This algorithm creates a list where each item is the best combination we have found so far for a specific size.

As we go through each test, we update the list from right to left. This way, we do not count the test more than once. At each step, the algorithm checks the best result with a new possible result that includes the current test. If the new result is better, we replace the solution. By the time we are done, the last item in the list is the combination of tests. The dynamic programming algorithm gives us the result by checking all possible combinations, but it is much faster. We wrote the program in Java. Used tools, like ArrayList, to store data. We. Tested the program using a special tool to make sure it works correctly. The program is fast because it only looks at the data once, and it can handle a lot of tests and a large amount of data.

	The accuracy of our implementation was ensured by comparison between the output of the greedy algorithms and that of the brute-force algorithm, which is used as a benchmark. The brute-force method considers all subsets, thus finding the best solution.

Some edge cases we considered include:

* Experiments that have very small weights but large ratings  
* Experiments that weigh close to the 700 kg capacity limit  
* Cases where the addition of a valuable experiment makes it impossible to accommodate other smaller experiments

One of the cross-checks we performed included verifying that none of the solutions exceeded the maximum 700 kg capacity while checking that the output of the dynamic programming algorithm is the same as the optimal brute-force output.

**Results**

![][image1]

![][image2]

We tested four approaches against the same data from the 12 experiments, each with a weight \+ rating, with a maximum shuttle capacity of 700 kg. The three greedy strategies each made selections one at a time based on a single bit of criteria: highest rating first, lightest weight first, and finally best rating-to-weight ratio first. Brute force tested all 4096 possible subsets and identified the top three valid results. Dynamic programming also found the optimal rating of 53 and actually selected the same set of experiments as brute force, which confirms that both methods are finding the correct optimal. The full output for each strategy is shown in the figures above.

Comparison Table 

| Strategy | Total Weight | Total Rating | Optimal? |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 3 Greedy: Highest Rating | 680 kg | 35 | No |
| 3 Greedy: Lightest First | 654 kg | 52  | No |
| 3 Greedy: Rating to Weight  | 654 kg  | 52 | No |
| Brute Force Top Result | 692 kg | 53  | Yes |
| Brute Force Second Result  | 654 kg  | 52 | No |
| Brute Force Third Result  | 687 kg  | 51  | No |
| Dynamic Programming  | 692 kg  | 53  | Yes |

**Discussion / Analysis**

As shown in the table, none of the greedy strategies hit the optimal rating of 53\. LightestFirst and RatingtoWeight Ratio both got to 52, which is pretty close, but HighestRatingFirst was the real poor performer at 35\. This strategy selected Solar Flares at 264 kg first thing, burning through more than a third of the weight budget on one experiment and leaving no room for the lighter high-value experiments that actually make up the optimal subset. This is a good example of how one bad early choice can tank the whole result. Brute force and dynamic programming both found the true optimal at 53, both selecting the same eight experiments with a combined weight of 692 kg. With BruteForce, it takes 4096 tries to find the best combination, which is 2 to the power of 12\. Scaling this, adding experiments to have 30 options would give BruteForce 2 to the power of 30 (1,073,741,824) combinations to try, and it only gets worse. 

Dynamic programming is the best choice here for scalability since it doesn’t need to test every possible combination; instead, it works by building up a solution one experiment at a time. It keeps an array where each slot represents a weight capacity, and for each experiment, it asks whether adding it would improve the best rating at that capacity. It works backwards through the array, so the same experiment does not get counted twice. By the end, all experiments will have been considered. Now the last slot holds the best possible rating within the weight limit, and that can be returned. 

One alternative approach I found is called meet in the middle (Wikipedia, Knapsack problem). Instead of brute-forcing all 4096 subsets, split the experiments into two groups of 6 and brute-force each half separately. That is just 2^6 (64) subsets per group. Once the best combination is found, add it back together. It was also interesting learning here in the write-up on Wiki that the knapsack problem is actually NP-complete, meaning there is no known algorithm that is both accurate and fast as the problem scales up. Dynamic programming is pseudopolynomial, which works fine here, but if the weight capacity were very large, it would start to struggle too.

**Conclusion**

In this regard, different methods of algorithmic implementation could be considered in solving the same optimization task with varying effectiveness and accuracy. Whereas the greedy algorithms offered quick and straightforward solutions, the results might not have been the most effective. On the other hand, the brute force approach was able to produce the optimum solution; however, it involved considerable effort in the computation process. In turn, the method based on dynamic programming allowed for finding the optimal solution efficiently.

**Summary of Work Done / Lessons Learned**

Doing this project helped our team see how different algorithms work on the same problem. Implementing the greedy strategies was not too difficult, but the results showed us the problems with making "locally optimal" decisions. For example, the highest-rating-first approach only got a score of 35, which was much lower than the optimal result.

The brute-force solution was good to have because it proved the exact answer. By checking all 4,096 subsets, we found a reliable benchmark to test our other methods against. Dynamic programming was the hardest part of the assignment. We had to research different ways to solve it and eventually found that our task was related to the knapsack problem.

The most interesting thing we learned is that the knapsack problem is NP-complete. This means no algorithm can stay fast and also be 100% optimal as the input size grows. Because of this, we realized that every approach we used is really a trade-off. This project helped us understand why the structure of a problem decides which algorithm to use. We also saw why brute force is rarely the right choice for larger data, even though it is simple and correct.
